title: Probiotics for prevention of recurrent Clostridioides difficile infection: a meta-analysis
authors: Li X, Wang J, Zhao T
abstract: BACKGROUND & AIMS: Recurrent C. difficile infection (rCDI) affects 20-30% of patients. We conducted a meta-analysis of RCTs evaluating probiotics for rCDI prevention. METHODS: 12 RCTs (N=3,847) were included. RESULTS: Probiotics reduced rCDI risk (RR 0.55, 95% CI 0.40-0.76, p<0.001, I2=35%). NNT was 10. Lactobacillus-based formulations showed the strongest effect (RR 0.42, 95% CI 0.27-0.66). Multi-strain products were more effective than single-strain (RR 0.48 vs 0.67). Adverse events were similar between groups (8.2% vs 7.9%, p=0.81). CONCLUSIONS: Probiotics significantly reduce rCDI, especially Lactobacillus-based multi-strain formulations.

title: Fecal microbiota transplantation vs vancomycin for recurrent C. difficile: 5-year follow-up
Saccharomyces boulardii for Clostridium difficile-associated diarrhoea in adults
abstract: BACKGROUND: S. boulardii has been proposed as adjunctive treatment for C. difficile-associated diarrhoea (CDAD). OBJECTIVES: To assess efficacy and safety of S. boulardii for treatment or prevention of CDAD. SEARCH METHODS: Cochrane Library, MEDLINE, EMBASE searched to 2009. SELECTION CRITERIA: RCTs of S. boulardii vs placebo/no treatment for CDAD. MAIN RESULTS: 5 RCTs included (N=1,076). S. boulardii reduced recurrence (RR 0.59, 95% CI 0.38-0.92). Effect was significant for recurrent CDAD (RR 0.43, 95% CI 0.22-0.87) but not initial episodes (RR 0.80, 95% CI 0.44-1.44). AUTHORS' CONCLUSIONS: Moderate evidence supports S. boulardii for preventing recurrent CDAD.
